阿里扎达是一名煤矿工人的女儿，她的家人搬到了阿富汗各地，为的是寻求安全和她和她的兄弟姐妹接受良好教育的机会。两年前，喀布尔一家辅导中心发生自杀式炸弹袭击，导致数十名同学死亡，阿里扎达是幸运儿之一。
但是，如果说是运气救了18岁的阿里扎达，那么，当周四电视上宣布她在阿富汗全国大学入学考试中取得了近20万名学生中的最高分时，正是她的韧性和勤奋激励了全国。她的母亲看到了，就亲自把这个喜讯告诉了她。
“我还以为她在开玩笑呢。但当我走进房间时，我看到母亲脸上露出了最灿烂的笑容，“阿里扎达女士在接受采访时说。“我见过她微笑，但昨天的微笑是另一回事。她的微笑是一份礼物，让我一天都很开心；这比获得全国最高分要好。“。
一代人以前，她可能永远不会有这个机会。在塔利班统治下，女孩被禁止上学。正是阿里扎达和像她这样的年轻阿富汗人的成功，提供了数十年战争中为数不多的亮点之一，也是自那以来西方国家未实现的目标之一：更多的女孩不仅在上学，她们还开始将其转化为社会流动性。
她或她的家人没有忘记，他们的欢乐时刻对他们的国家来说也是一个关键的时刻。阿富汗政府和塔利班的谈判代表正在进行谈判，寻求结束战争，试图塑造一个能够保持和平的权力分享政府。
在全国各地，阿富汗妇女担心这也可能意味着回归镇压。
“我们经历了很多，”阿里扎达说。“我希望塔利班让阿富汗妇女实现她们的梦想。我们不想受到限制。“。
阿里扎达当时是一家辅导中心的大三学生，该中心为阿富汗竞争激烈的大学入学考试做准备。当时，一名自杀式炸弹袭击者走进挤满了200多名大年级学生的演讲厅，引爆了他的爆炸背心。
房间里一半的学生被杀或受伤，阿里扎达失去了她尊敬的朋友和年轻女性。一个孕育着普遍梦想的中心-良好的教育是摆脱贫困和压迫的门票-变成了一场屠杀的场景，白板上的代数方程式沾满了鲜血。
这种暴力每天都在结束年轻的、充满希望的阿富汗人的生命，甚至在塔利班开始强加条件之前就扼杀了妇女和少数民族的梦想。从本质上讲，阿富汗的极端分子控制着这一等式的两边-保持暴力，以帮助他们现在谈判中的地位，同时坚持他们稍后恢复坚定不移的伊斯兰统治的目标。
塔利班的谈判代表对他们目前在女性教育问题上的具体立场仍然含糊其辞，几乎没有提供细节，但表示他们尊重女性的受教育和工作权，“根据伊斯兰教法”-许多女性担心这一警告会使她们的成果变得脆弱。
尽管美国吹捧女孩地位的改善是其在阿富汗存在的重大成功，但在与塔利班签署的美军撤军协议中，美国没有提到对她们的保护。这样的问题留给阿富汗双方进行谈判。
然而，美国政府已经明确表示，将继续利用其对阿富汗的数十亿美元援助，作为推动双方尊重公民自由成果的筹码。阿富汗大约75%的支出依赖外国援助。
“我希望双方都能兑现承诺--我希望不再有人在阿富汗被杀，”阿里扎达说。
教育机会的扩大一直是美国及其盟友20年来对阿富汗进行混乱干预的一个快乐故事。
当塔利班在2001年被推翻时，学校里只有大约100万名学生，几乎都是男性。由于贫困，基础设施已经很薄弱，在数十年的冲突中遭到了严重破坏。
20年的投资使招生人数增加了9倍。私立大学在全国城市中心如雨后春笋般涌现。
但和其他许多事情一样，教育工作也受到了严重腐败的破坏，官方数字经常受到质疑。近年来，暴力事件也造成了严重的伤亡，1000多所学校因袭击事件而关闭。根据联合国儿童基金会的数据，三分之一的学龄儿童仍然失学。
伊斯兰国的一个分支声称对发生在喀布尔马乌德学院(Mawoud Academy)的爆炸事件负责，该分支利用阿富汗复杂的战场，袭击了清真寺、学校和医院等软目标。阿里扎达曾就读于该学院。演讲厅人满为患，爆炸威力巨大，造成至少40人死亡，60多人受伤。
大多数受害者是来自阿富汗中部小村庄的贫困儿童，那里是该国以哈扎拉族为主的地区。许多人住在首都喀布尔，住在远离家人的每月15美元的宿舍里，以便继续接受教育。他们的一些棺材被运回了村庄。其他人，包括一对抱着19岁双胞胎的人，最终被埋在喀布尔的山顶上。
Alizada女士是哈扎拉人，她来自加兹尼省的Jaghori区，但她的五口之家从婴儿时代起就一直在寻找更好的生活。在搬到喀布尔之前，她的家人住在赫拉特省西部。在赫拉特，阿里扎达参加了免费的武术课程，但她不得不放弃在喀布尔的训练，因为那里的课程太贵了。
她的父亲是阿富汗北部的一名煤矿工人，每六个月才回家一次。
“萨曼干的电话连接真的很差，”阿里扎达说，她指的是她父亲工作的北部省份。“昨晚8点，我终于设法和父亲谈了谈。他高兴得流下了眼泪。“
note_en:
A coal miner’s daughter whose family had moved all around Afghanistan seeking safety and the chance for her and her siblings to get a good education, Ms. Alizada was among the lucky who evaded the  suicide bombing that killed dozens of her fellow students at a Kabul tutoring center two years ago.
 But if it was luck that saved Ms. Alizada, 18, it was resilience and hard work that made her a national inspiration, when it was announced on television on Thursday that she had achieved the highest score out of nearly 200,000 students on Afghanistan’s national university entrance exam. Her mother saw it and gave her the happy news personally.
 “I thought she was kidding. But when I entered the room, I saw the brightest smile on my mother’s face,” Ms. Alizada said in an interview. “I have seen her smiling, but yesterday’s smile was something else. Her smile was a gift and made my day; it was better than gaining the highest score in the country.”
 A generation ago, she would probably never have gotten the chance. Under Taliban rule, girls were prevented from going to school. It is the success of Ms. Alizada and young Afghans like her that have provided one of the few bright spots in the decades of war and unrealized Western goals since: More girls are not only going to school, they are also starting to translate that into social mobility.
 It is not lost on her or her family that their joyful moment is also a critical one for their country. Negotiators for  the Afghan government and the Taliban are engaged in talks seeking an end to the war, trying to shape a power-sharing government that can keep peace.
 Around the country,  Afghan women are worried that it could also mean a return to repression.
 “We have been through a lot,” Ms. Alizada said. “I hope the Taliban let the Afghan women live their dreams. We don’t want to be limited.”
 Ms. Alizada was a junior student at a tutoring center that prepared underprivileged Afghans for the country’s competitive university entrance exam when  a suicide bomber walked into a lecture hall packed with more than 200 older students and detonated his explosive vest.
 Half of the students in the room were killed or wounded, and Ms. Alizada lost friends and young women she looked up to. A center that incubated a universal dream — a good education as the ticket out of poverty and oppression — was turned into a scene of carnage, the algebra equations on the whiteboard covered in blood.
 That kind of violence keeps ending young, hopeful Afghan lives every day, curtailing the dreams of women and minorities even before the Taliban can start imposing terms. Extremists in Afghanistan, in essence, control both sides of that equation — keeping up the violence to help their position in talks now, while holding to their goal of a return to an unbending Islamic rule later.
  The Taliban’s negotiators have remained vague on where exactly they stand on women’s education now, offering little detail but to say that they respect women’s right to education and work “according to Islamic Shariah” — a caveat many women fear leaves their gains vulnerable.
 The United States, although touting the improvement in the status of girls as a major success of its presence in Afghanistan, made no mention of their protection in the deal it signed with the Taliban over withdrawing American troops. Such issues are left for negotiating between the Afghan sides.
 The U.S. government, however, has made clear it will continue to use its billions of dollars in aid to Afghanistan, which depends on foreign assistance for roughly 75 percent of its spending, as leverage to push the sides to respect gains in civil liberties.
 “I hope both sides fulfill their promise — I hope no one gets killed in Afghanistan anymore,” Ms. Alizada said.
 The expansion of education opportunities has been a happy story of the chaotic, two-decade intervention in Afghanistan by the United States and its allies.
 When the Taliban were toppled in 2001, there were just about 1 million students in schools, almost all male. The infrastructure, already weak because of poverty, was badly damaged by decades of conflict.
 Twenty years of investment increased enrollment up to ninefold. Private universities have popped up across the country’s urban centers.
 But like many other things, the education effort has been marred by deep corruption, and  the official numbers have often come into question. In recent years, violence has also taken a deep toll, with more than 1,000 schools shut because of attacks. According to UNICEF,  a third of all school-aged children remain out of school.
 The bombing at Mawoud Academy in Kabul, which Ms. Alizada attended, was claimed by an offshoot of the Islamic State that has exploited the complicated battlefield in Afghanistan, attacking soft targets like mosques, schools and hospitals. The lecture hall had been so packed, and the explosion so powerful, that at least 40 people were killed and more than 60 others wounded.
 Most of the victims were underprivileged children from small villages in central Afghanistan, a largely ethnic Hazara part of the country. Many stayed in Kabul, the capital, at $15-a-month hostels away from their families in order to pursue their educations. Some of their coffins made it back to the villages. Others, including a pair holding 19-year-old twins, ended up buried on a Kabul hilltop.
 Ms. Alizada, who is Hazara, is originally from the Jaghori district of Ghazni Province, but her family of five has been on the move in search of a better life since she was an infant. Her family lived in western Herat Province before moving to Kabul. In Herat, Ms. Alizada took free martial arts classes, but she had to give up her training in Kabul, as the lessons there were too expensive.
 Her father is a coal miner in northern Afghanistan, making it home only every six months.
 “The phone connection in Samangan is really bad,” Ms. Alizada said, referring to the northern province where her father works. “Last night at 8 p.m., I finally managed to talk to my father. He was so happy he was in tears.”
title: 她的辅导中心被炸毁了。她在阿富汗的大学入学考试中名列前茅
title_en: Her tutoring center was bombed. She leads Afghanistan's university entrance exam

旧金山(路透社)-Facebook Inc.周五表示，在2020年剩余时间里，在其iOS应用程序上开展付费在线活动的企业将不需要向苹果支付30%的费用，暂时缓解了这两家科技巨头之间的僵局。
这家社交媒体公司在一篇博客文章中表示，除游戏创作者外，所有企业都有资格获得苹果的费用豁免，并可以处理他们通过Facebook Pay运营的在线活动的支付。
Facebook公司发言人乔·奥斯本(Joe Osborne)在一份声明中表示：“苹果已经同意提供短暂的三个月的喘息时间，之后陷入困境的企业将不得不再次向苹果支付全额30%的App Store税。”
Facebook表示，在企业因冠状病毒大流行而关闭期间，至少在2021年8月之前，它不会对在线活动收取自己的费用。
苹果周五表示，此类在线活动一直受其应用内支付规则的约束，这些规则收取付费在线活动购买价格的15%至30%之间的佣金。
苹果公司表示，它已经给了受疫情影响的企业更多的时间来实施该系统，Facebook也将获得同样的豁免，直到年底，它已经给予ClassPass和Airbnb同样的豁免。
苹果表示，游戏创建者不会获得豁免，因为这项服务是在2018年初推出的，而且它不是受疫情影响的实体业务。
苹果在一份声明中表示：“苹果保持着一套明确、一致的指导方针，对每个人都是平等的。”
Facebook上个月挑战了苹果的规则，试图在一次应用更新中告诉用户，这家iPhone制造商将从一项新的在线活动功能的销售额中提成，但在苹果拒绝更新后，Facebook后来删除了这条消息。
这家全球最大的社交媒体公司将此举描述为保护小企业和应用程序开发商，加入了其他开发商的行列，如“堡垒之夜”的创作者Epic Games，新浪微博正就这些费用起诉苹果公司。
Facebook还在与苹果就iPhone的新隐私规则发生争执，该规则将要求在跨应用程序跟踪用户之前发出更多通知。
这家社交媒体巨头表示，苹果提高收费的决定是有问题的，因为游戏创作者不能在iOS上的付费在线活动中使用Facebook Pay。
Facebook Gaming副总裁Vivek Sharma表示：“不幸的是，我们不得不做出这一让步，以获得其他业务的暂时喘息机会，”Facebook Gaming副总裁维韦克·夏尔马(Vivek Sharma)表示。
苹果公司正在为“堡垒之夜”创作者Epic Games就其应用内支付规则提起的反垄断诉讼进行辩护。
note_en:
SAN FRANCISCO (Reuters) - Facebook Inc said on Friday that businesses running paid online events on its iOS app would not need to pay a 30% fee to Apple for the remainder of 2020, temporarily defusing a standoff between the two tech giants.
  The social media company said in a blog post that all businesses except gaming creators would be eligible for Apple’s fee exemption and can process payments for the online events they run through Facebook Pay.
 “Apple has agreed to provide a brief, three-month respite after which struggling businesses will have to, yet again, pay Apple the full 30% App Store tax,” Facebook company spokesman Joe Osborne said in a statement.
 Facebook said it will not charge fees of its own for online events while businesses remain closed due to the coronavirus pandemic, through at least August 2021.
 Apple said Friday that such online events have always been subject to its in-app payment rules, which charge commissions between 15% and 30% of the purchase price of paid online events.
  Apple said it has given businesses affected by the pandemic more time to implement the system and that Facebook is getting the same exemption until year’s end it has given ClassPass and Airbnb.
 Gaming creators will not receive the exemption because the service was launched in early 2018 and it is not a physical business affected by the pandemic, Apple said.
 “Apple maintains a clear, consistent set of guidelines that apply equally to everyone,” Apple said in a statement.
 Facebook challenged Apple’s rules last month, attempting to tell users in an app update that the iPhone maker would take a cut of sales for a new online events feature, but later removed the message after Apple rejected the update.
  The world’s biggest social media company cast the move as a defense of small businesses and app developers, joining other developers such as “Fortnite” creator Epic Games, which is suing Apple on antitrust allegations over the fees.
 Facebook is also wrangling with Apple over new privacy rules for iPhones that will require more notifications before tracking users across apps.
 The social media giant said Apple’s decision to lift the fees came with a catch excluding game creators from being able to use Facebook Pay in paid online events on iOS.
 “We unfortunately had to make this concession to get the temporary reprieve for other businesses,” said Vivek Sharma, vice president of Facebook Gaming.
 Apple is defending an antitrust lawsuit brought by “Fortnite” creator Epic Games over its in-app payment rules.
title: Facebook Events服务获得苹果应用商店费用临时豁免
title_en: Facebook events service gets temporary exemption from Apple App Store fees

note:
艺术历史学家通常不会像肯尼斯·克拉克(Kenneth Clark)晚年那样成为家喻户晓的名字，因为他拥抱了电视，最著名的是“文明”(1969年)。然而，他们可以在职业和业余爱好者中获得传奇或半传奇的地位。利奥·斯坦伯格(Leo Steinberg，1920-2011)就是其中之一，他曾在纽约城市大学和宾夕法尼亚大学任教多年。
预计学术艺术史学家将“要么发表，要么灭亡”：大学课本、关于艺术家个人的专题研究，或者关于奥秘的地下论文，这些论文似乎需要更多的脚注，而不是文本。斯坦伯格主要为专业期刊撰稿。他还进行了广泛的演讲，测试以后可能会付印的想法。他是一位富有魅力的演说家，甚至是戏剧性的演说家，据说他在笔记中加入了舞台说明：“停下来在这里点根烟。”
近年来，他的编辑助理希拉·施瓦茨(Sheila Schwartz)一直在努力赋予这些努力更持久的生命，本质上是短暂的。选集已经出现在米开朗基罗的雕塑和油画上。文艺复兴和巴洛克艺术是第三本，即将出版更多关于毕加索和现代艺术方面的书籍。
文艺复兴和巴洛克艺术包括迭戈·贝拉兹奎兹(Diego Velázquez)的“拉斯梅尼纳斯”(Las Meninas)等关于Keystone绘画的散文，以及埃尔·格列科(El Greco)、卡拉瓦乔(Caravaggio)和曼特纳(Mantegna)等艺术家的作品。由于斯坦伯格的散文风格几乎是对话式的-活泼、识字和通俗易懂-而且他的洞察力几乎不亚于启示性，所以这里有很多适合普通读者的东西。
自相矛盾的是，对于一个植根于视觉的职业来说，大多数艺术历史学家往往是读者，而不是旁观者。因为关于约会、归属、解释等的观点必须以遗嘱、合同和信件等纪录片的存续为基础，所以学者们往往不太重视站在艺术品面前所学到的东西，而更看重他们在图书馆里学到的东西。
斯坦伯格则不同。他做了背景阅读并梳理了档案，但他的“文本”是作品本身，解读其含义的主要方法是长时间仔细查看。这种坚持认为他对作品意义的理解是建立在经验的基础上的，这导致他做了一些事情，比如采用被描绘的人物的姿势，以便更好地理解艺术家的意图。这种观点无疑就是为什么没有其他艺术历史学家对西方主要艺术作品提出更开创性的重新解读，无论是米开朗基罗的“皮埃塔”还是毕加索的“阿维尼翁的德莫伊塞尔”(Les Demoiselle d‘Avinon)。
以马德里普拉多博物馆的贝拉兹克斯的“拉斯梅尼纳斯”(约1656年)为例。这幅不朽的画作(大约10英尺乘9英寸)几乎被普遍认为是有史以来最令人着迷的画作。有一种方式颠倒了事物的正常秩序：我们不是看着画中的人物，而是他们看着我们。它有复杂的结构和前景人物，艺术家在Infanta，她的侍女和宫廷矮人之间工作。在后面很远的地方有一个人物，站在通往更深空间的门口，他是宫廷总管唐·何塞·涅托·贝拉兹奎斯(Don JoséNieto Velázquez)，可能是这位艺术家的亲戚；在内庭旁边有一面镜子，框着两个人物：菲利普四世国王(King Philip IV)和他的奥地利第二任妻子玛丽安娜(Mariana)，他们大概就站在我们旁边或附近。最后，还有它的两个谜团：这里到底发生了什么？这一切又意味着什么呢？
在斯坦伯格之前，分析围绕着两条线索进行。Velázquez的上衣上印有圣地亚哥红十字会的标志，标志着他是一名贵族，他正在皇室成员面前练习他的艺术。第一条线索使这部作品成为关于绘画艺术高贵的寓言，直到不久前，它还被认为充其量是手工艺，最糟糕的是纯粹的体力劳动。第二种观点认为，这部作品是关于我们这些观众的。
斯坦伯格首先宣布这种方法过于狭隘。“我想念的是…。是与整幅画…必要的接触吗？有不成比例的画布面积仍然下落不明。然后，他探索这片土地。他盘点了它的多个透视系统；大多数照片只有一个，但这张有四个。他对这九个数字做了同样的处理，发现它们在平面图上被布置在三个三角形中。
然后他发现了第三个三角形，它的点由倒影中的国王和王后在背后深处界定，我们站在外面。不过，他说，倒影是维拉兹克斯正在创作的油画的一部分，使其成为一种错觉。既然Velázquez正在看着我们，这意味着真正的国王和王后正站在我们旁边摆姿势。所有这一切加在一起，就是一个关于艺术是如何制作、如何观看以及如何制作以供观看的综合理论：“画家给了我们真实的、反映的和描绘的三种相互依存的状态…。在一个永恒的轮回中彼此起因并取得成功。
如果所有这些看起来让你头脑发热，那么，这是为你准备的艺术。

note_en:
Art historians do not generally become household names, as Kenneth Clark did later in life after embracing television, most famously with  Civilisation (1969). They can, however, acquire legendary or semilegendary status within the profession and among  amateurs. One such was Leo Steinberg (1920-2011), who taught for many years at the City University of New York and the University of Pennsylvania.
 Academic art historians are expected to ‘publish or perish’: college texts, monographic studies on individual artists, or down-therabbit-hole treatises on arcana that seem to require more pages of footnotes than text. Steinberg mainly wrote for professional journals. He also lectured widely, testing ideas that later might make their way into print. A charismatic, even theatrical speaker, he is said to have included stage directions in his notes: ‘Pause to light cigarette here.’
 In recent years, his editorial assistant Sheila Schwartz has been working to give these efforts, by nature ephemeral, a more permanent life. Anthologies have already appeared on Michelangelo’s sculpture and his painting.   Renaissance and Baroque Art is the third, with further volumes on Picasso and aspects of modern art forthcoming.
 Renaissance and Baroque Art includes essays on keystone paintings like Diego Velázquez’s ‘Las Meninas’, as well as works by artists such as El Greco, Caravaggio and Mantegna. Because Steinberg’s prose style is almost conversational — lively, literate and accessible — and his insights rarely less than revelatory, there’s plenty here for the general reader.
  Paradoxically for members of a profession rooted in the visual, most art historians tend to be readers rather than lookers. Because opinions about dating, attribution, interpretation and the like must be grounded in documentary survivals such as wills, contracts and letters, scholars tend to give less weight to what they have learned about a work of art by standing in front it, and more to what they have learned about it in the library.
 Steinberg was different. He did the background reading and combed the archives, but his ‘text’ was the work itself, and the primary method of unpacking its meaning was long, close looking. This insistence that his understanding of a work’s meaning be grounded in empirical experience led him to do such things as adopt the pose of a depicted figure, the better to understand the artist’s intention. This outlook is surely why no other art historian has advanced more groundbreaking reinterpretations of major works of Western art, be it Michelangelo’s ‘Pietà’ or Picasso’s ‘Les Demoiselles d’Avignon’.
 Take Velázquez’s ‘Las Meninas’ ( c. 1656), in the Prado Museum, Madrid. This monumental painting (roughly 10’ x 9’) is almost universally considered to be the most bewitching ever made. There is the way it reverses the normal order of things: instead of us looking at painted figures, they look out at us. There is its complex construction with foreground figures, with the artist at work among the Infanta, her  meninas (ladies-in-waiting) and the court dwarves. There is a figure far in the back, standing in a doorway opening onto still deeper space, the court chamberlain Don José Nieto Velázquez, possibly a relation of the artist’s; and, next to the chamberlain, a mirror framing two figures: King Philip IV and his Austrian second wife, Mariana, who presumably are standing next to or near us. Finally, there are its twin enigmas: just what is going on here? And what does it all mean?
 Before Steinberg, analysis coalesced around two clues. Velázquez’s doublet is emblazoned with the red cross of the Order of Santiago, marking him as an aristocrat, and he’s practicing his art in the presence of royalty. The first clue makes the work an allegory on the nobility of the art of painting, until not too long before regarded at best as craft and at worst as mere manual labor. The second holds that the work is about us, the viewers.
 Steinberg begins by declaring that approach too narrow. ‘What I miss…is the necessary engagement with the whole painting…a disproportionate acreage of the canvas remains unaccounted for.’ He then explores that acreage. He inventories its multiple perspectival systems; most pictures have just one, but this has four. He does the same with the nine figures and discovers they are disposed, in plan, in three triangles.
 Then he finds a third triangle, its points defined by the reflected king and queen deep in the back, with us standing outside. Except, he says, the reflection is of part of the painting Velázquez is working on, making it an illusion of an illusion. And since Velázquez is looking out at us, it means the real king and queen are standing next to us, posing. What it all adds up to, then, is a comprehensive theory of how art is made and seen, and made to be seen: ‘The painter gives us the real, the reflected and the depicted as three interdependent states…that cause and succeed one another in a perpetual round.’
 If all this seems a lot to get your head around, well, that’s art for you.

note:
Alphabet已与股东在一起诉讼中达成和解，指控包括前首席法务官大卫·德拉蒙德(David Drummond)在内的高管对性行为不当处理和掩盖。
该公司将取消强制性仲裁，限制谷歌使用保密协议，同时禁止谷歌向任何因性骚扰、性行为不当或报复而被解雇的员工提供遣散费，包括一名高管。
这项和解协议是在谷歌母公司Alphabet董事会去年成立独立董事特别诉讼委员会调查这些指控近一年后达成的。
Alphabet已就一项股东诉讼达成和解，该诉讼指控董事会对其高管的性行为不当处理不当。
根据原告律师提供的和解摘要，高管在接受调查或因性行为不当而提起诉讼期间，将无法获得遣散费或修改他们的股票销售计划。和解协议还取消了强制性仲裁，并限制谷歌对涉及这些案件的员工使用保密协议。
它还包括3.1亿美元的承诺，用于资助和创建一个由外部专家组成的多样性、公平和包容性咨询委员会。
在宣布和解的帖子中，谷歌人力运营主管艾琳·诺顿(Eileen Naughton)表示，公司承诺遵守五项新的指导原则和一系列详细的变化。
和解协议防止拥有10b5-1股票购买计划的员工在因性行为不当或性骚扰接受调查期间修改这些计划。例如，这将阻止接受调查的高管加快股票销售。
Alphabet将承诺在10年内为Dei Council以及多样性、公平性和包容性倡议提供3.1亿美元的资金。
咨询委员会包括传奇法官南希·格特纳(Nancy Gertner)、前平等就业机会委员会委员弗雷德·阿尔瓦雷斯(Fred Alvarez)和就业律师格蕾丝·斯皮茨(Grace Speights)等外部专家，以及将于第一年参加的首席执行官桑达尔·皮查伊(Sundar Pichai)等内部领导人。
和解协议要求Alphabet修改其领导力发展和薪酬委员会(Leadership Development And Compensation Committee)的领导力章程-该委员会批准向谷歌前高管安迪·鲁宾(Andy Rubin)和阿米特·辛格尔(Amit Singhal)支付薪酬，以监督有关性骚扰、歧视和报复指控的报告和解决方案的数据。它还需要向董事会报告可能涉及不当行为指控的高管的任何薪酬决定。
在限制谷歌使用保密协议方面，Alphabet的员工，包括其其他博彩部门，在了结索赔后，将能够讨论涉嫌骚扰、歧视或不当行为的事实和情况。
诺顿说，在过去的几年里，我们对不当行为采取了更强硬的立场，并努力为举报这一行为的人提供更好的支持。保护我们的工作场所和文化意味着要把这两件事都做好，近年来，我们努力为整个公司设定和维护更高的标准。
2019年初，律师代表一名公司股东对Alphabet董事会提起诉讼，指控其包庇高管免受性行为不当指控，指控其违反受托责任、滥用控制权、不当得利和浪费公司资产。
据报道，谷歌向安卓领导人安迪·鲁宾支付了9000万美元的离职方案，尽管在发现针对他的性行为不当指控可信后，谷歌要求他辞职，这导致了全球员工罢工，并在2018年修改了一些与性行为不当相关的政策。
据CNBC去年11月首次报道，谷歌母公司Alphabet董事会去年成立了独立董事特别诉讼委员会(Special Litigation Committee)，并聘请克拉瓦特·斯瓦恩·摩尔律师事务所(Cravath，Swine&Amore；Moore)对高管的性行为不端行为进行调查。近一年前，谷歌达成了和解。
调查包括Alphabet首席法务官大卫·德拉蒙德(David Drummond)的行为，德拉蒙德是当时薪酬最高的高管之一，他在1月份不久后就从公司退休了。
这项和解协议很可能会在Alphabet带来重大的、变革性的变革方面取得持久、长期的成功，因为在原告提起诉讼后，许多推动者和肇事者被迫退出或干脆离开公司：首席法务官大卫·德拉蒙德(David Drummond)和埃里克·施密特(Eric Schmidt)离开了董事会。德拉蒙德是谷歌双重标准的缩影，他对公司关系政策的不受惩罚的违规行为就是他的代表。埃里克·施密特的公开事务和藐视公司政策为谷歌的高管定下了基调。这两个人是：首席法务官大卫·德拉蒙德(David Drummond)和埃里克·施密特(Eric Schmidt)-他公开事务和藐视公司政策为谷歌的高管定下了基调--他对公司关系政策的未受惩罚的违规行为是谷歌双重标准的缩影。
note_en:
Alphabet has reached a settlement with shareholders in a lawsuit alleging sexual misconduct mishandling and cover-up by executives, including former chief legal officer David Drummond.
 The company will eliminate mandatory arbitration and limit Google's use of non-disclosure agreements while prohibiting Google from "providing severance to any employee, including a senior executive, terminated for sexual harassment, sexual misconduct, or retaliation."
 The settlement comes nearly one year after the board of Google parent company Alphabet formed a Special Litigation Committee of independent directors last year to investigate the claims.
 Alphabet has reached a settlement on a shareholder lawsuit that accused the board of allegedly mishandling sexual misconduct by its executives.
 Execs will not be able to receive severance or amend their stock sale plans while they are while they are subject to investigations or a lawsuit for sexual misconduct, according to a summary of the settlement provided by the plaintiffs' attorneys. The settlement also eliminates mandatory arbitration and limits Google's use of non-disclosure agreements for employees involved in these cases.
  It also includes a $310 million commitment to fund and create a diversity, equity and inclusion advisory council comprised of outside experts.
 In a  post announcing the settlement, Google's head of people operations Eileen Naughton said the company is committing to  five new guiding principles and a list of detailed changes.
  The Settlement prevents employees with 10b5-1 stock purchase plans from amending them while under investigation for sexual misconduct or harassment. This would prevent, for example, an executive under investigation from accelerating sales of stock.
 Alphabet will commit $310 million to fund the DEI Council and diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives over 10 years.
 The Advisory Council includes outside experts including legendary Judge Nancy Gertner, former EEOC Commissioner Fred Alvarez, and employment lawyer Grace Speights as well as internal leaders, including CEO Sundar Pichai who will participate for the first year.
 The settlement requires Alphabet to amend its leadership charters for its Leadership Development and Compensation Committee — the committee that approved payouts to former Google executives Andy Rubin and Amit Singhal — to oversee data regarding reports and resolutions of claims of sexual harassment, discrimination and retaliation. It will also need to report to the board any compensation decisions for senior executives who may have engaged with claims of misconduct.
 On limiting Google's use of non-disclosure agreements, employees of Alphabet, including its Other Bets segments, who settle claims will be able to discuss facts and circumstances of alleged harassment, discrimination or wrongdoing.
 "Over the past several years, we have been taking a harder line on inappropriate conduct, and have worked to provide better support to the people who report it," Naughton stated. "Protecting our workplace and culture means getting both of these things right, and in recent years we've worked hard to set and uphold higher standards for the whole company."
 In early 2019, attorneys  filed a lawsuit against Alphabet's board of director's on behalf of a company shareholder for allegedly shielding senior execs from accusations of sexual misconduct, claiming a breach of fiduciary duty, abuse of control, unjust enrichment and waste of corporate assets.
  Google reportedly paid Android leader  Andy Rubin a $90 million exit package, despite asking for his resignation after finding sexual misconduct claims against him credible,  which led to a global walkout of employees  and the amendment of some of its policies relating to sexual misconduct in 2018.
 The settlement comes nearly one year after the board of Google parent company Alphabet formed a Special Litigation Committee of independent directors last year and hired the law firm Cravath, Swaine & Moore to conduct an investigation into sexual misconduct by executives, CNBC  first reported last November.
 The investigation encompassed behavior by Alphabet's Chief Legal Officer David Drummond — one of the highest paid executives at the time — who ended up  retiring from the company shortly after, in January.
  "This settlement is likely to have lasting, long-term success in bringing about major, transformative changes at Alphabet because, subsequent to the filing of Plaintiffs' lawsuit, many of the enablers and perpetrators were forced to step back or leave the Company altogether: Chief Legal Officer David Drummond—whose unpunished violations of the company's relationships policy epitomized Google's double-standard—resigned, and Eric Schmidt—whose open affairs and flouting of company policies set the tone for Google's executives—left the Board."
title: Alphabet就其处理性骚扰指控的股东诉讼达成和解，同意取消强制性仲裁并限制谷歌使用NDA
